scenario: Junction Utility runs an end-of-day billing process in Camunda 8.8 that must start a new process instance every weekday at 18:00 across 7000 customers. The process also contains a wait state that must elapse 30 minutes before a reminder branch fires.
question: Which timer expression types are correct for the two timer roles described?

options.text: Use a cycle expression on the timer start event and a duration expression on the intermediate timer event for the 30 minutes wait
Use a date expression on the timer start event and a cycle expression on the intermediate timer event to fire it every 30 minutes
Use a duration expression on the timer start event and a date expression on the intermediate timer event for the 30 minutes wait
Use a cycle expression on both events; one anchored to weekdays and the other anchored to a window of 30 minutes per instance

a.text: Correct. Cycle expressions trigger repeating starts (e.g., weekdays 18:00) and duration expressions fit a one-shot delay on an intermediate timer event.

b.text: Incorrect. A date expression fires once at an absolute timestamp and cannot recur every weekday for a timer start event.

c.text: Incorrect. A duration expression on a start event would only fire once after deployment, not on every weekday.

d.text: Incorrect. A cycle on an intermediate timer event creates a repeating timer, not a single 30 minutes wait per process instance.

explanation: Camunda timer events accept date, duration, and cycle expressions; cycle drives repeating starts and duration drives one-shot intermediate waits.
